exemplar.title: To what extent have wildfires in california increased PM2.5 levels, contributing to a rise in current asthma rates from 2000 to 2020?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent have wildfires in california increased PM2.5 levels, contributing to a rise in current asthma rates from 2000 to 2020?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Report follows standard IB IA structure with clear headings and figures
Argument generally progresses logically from context to results and evaluation
weaknesses: Formatting glitches and duplicated captions disrupt flow
Inconsistent figure numbering and occasional incoherent transitions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure, terminology and logic each align with bandâ2 performanceâpresent but inconsistently sustained. The work is sufficiently organized and coherent to merit 2/3, but pervasive formatting and clarity issues prevent a higher score.

strengths: Formulates a precise, focused research question specifying variables, location and timeframe
Provides a clear, wellâdeveloped environmental context with authoritative sources
Explains the connections between wildfire area, PM2.5 levels and asthma prevalence comprehensively
weaknesses: Minor duplication in phrasing of the research question reduces polish
Could more directly integrate climateâdriven drought link to wildfire frequency
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments consistently placed each subâcriterion in the top band (5/6), with clear demonstration of a focused question, rich context and explained connections. Minor stylistic issues (duplication, slight repetition) prevent a full 6, so 5 accurately reflects the high quality and coherence of the contextualization.

strengths: Method design is repeatable and uses appropriate secondary datasets and statistical tests
Sampling frame is clearly described (California averages, 2000â2020)
Ethical considerations and dataâaccuracy obligations are acknowledged
weaknesses: Justification for choosing statewide averages and addressing spatial bias is lacking
No control strategies for confounding variables are defined
Risk and ethical analysis is superficial, missing dataâsecurity and privacy discussion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The method strand fulfills basic repeatability (4/6), sampling is described without justification (3/6) and ethics are only listed (2/6). Overall performance aligns with the midâband, so a holistic score of 3/6 appropriately balances strengths and omissions.

strengths: Evaluates the conclusion in environmental context with reference to data reliability
Describes multiple strengths and weaknesses of the method
Suggests meaningful improvements and further research avenues
weaknesses: Does not address contradictory or nonâsignificant findings in evaluation
Method discussion omits statistical assumption checks
Improvement proposals lack detail on implementation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each subâstrand meets the âdescribes/evaluates with omissionsâ descriptor at the lower end of band 3â4, warranting 3/6. Coverage is present but incomplete, and depth is limited, so this holistic score reflects moderate evaluation and extension.

strengths: All raw data are presented with charts and trendlines for each variable pair
Correct calculation and interpretation of Pearsonâs r and pâvalues
Conclusion restates key numerical finding and links to the research question
weaknesses: Graphical presentation has labeling, numbering and formatting errors
Nonâsignificant results are not interpreted or discussed
Rounding conventions and data presentation lack consistency
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation and analysis each sit at the top of band 3â4 (4/6), but conclusion drawing is only partially supported (3/6). Given the omissions in interpretation and presentation errors, a score of 3/6 best reflects the cumulative quality of this section.

strengths: Proposes a relevant application (fireâresistant plants) linked to study findings
Discusses several strengths (mitigation potential) and weaknesses (cost, time) of the solution
weaknesses: Justification does not quantitatively link to data results
Evaluation omits broader ecological tradeâoffs and feasibility issues
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Application proposal is described with weak justification and evaluation covers only some strengths/weaknesses, matching the bandâ2 descriptors. A score of 2/3 accurately reflects the level of development and critique in this section.

strandSelections.reasoning: ESS terminology (PM2.5, Pearson correlation, p-value) is used correctly, yet verbosity, repetition and occasional grammatical errors impede concision.  The work therefore satisfies *either* criterion (terminology) but not both fully, placing it in band 2.

The evaluation section considers reliability of sources, p-value meaning and health implications, but overlooks the contradictory PM2.5 finding and broader environmental complexity.  Thus the conclusion is evaluated *with omissions*, fitting the 3â4 band; limited depth yields 3.

The conclusion restates the key numerical finding and links it to the research question, but does not integrate the non-significant PM2.5 results or address uncertainty, so support is partial.  This matches the descriptor âinterprets trends for a conclusion with some validityâ; the lower band mark (3) is appropriate.

The student correctly calculates r and p values, interprets significance, and comments on strength/direction for three data pairs.  Deeper statistical checks (normality, confounders) are absent and one paragraph mis-labels a negative correlation, so analysis is accurate but incompleteâsquarely within 3â4, top mark 4.

The student proposes several improvements (contact CARB, add primary interviews, focus on sub-regions).  Suggestions are practical and address noted weaknesses, but lack detail on implementation or extension of analysis techniques.  Therefore the work meets the âmeaningfulâ threshold (3â4) at the lower mark of 3.

The âmethodâ section specifies sources, time span, variables and statistical test, allowing replication with the same datasets, and the test chosen (Pearson r) is appropriate for detecting correlations in secondary data.  However, control of confounding variables is not addressed and justification for using statewide averages is weak, so the design, while repeatable, is merely *sufficient*, aligning with the middle band upper mark (4).

Links between the issue (wildfire-driven air pollution) and the research question are explained throughout: the student shows how burned area influences PM2.5, how PM2.5 can trigger asthma, and why analysing 2000â2020 data can reveal trends.  The argument is logical and covers all three variables, satisfying the requirement to *explain* connections.  Some statements remain descriptive rather than analytical, so 5/6.

The student formulates a precise, measurable and temporally bounded question that clearly identifies the three variables (wildfire area, PM2.5, asthma prevalence) and specifies location (California) and period (2000â2020).  The wording is coherent, free of ambiguity and demonstrates clear focus on causal links, fulfilling the descriptor for the highest band.  Minor stylistic flaws (two similar versions of the question) slightly reduce polish, so 5 rather than 6 is awarded.

Strengths (mitigates fires) and weaknesses (time, cost, not fire-proof) are discussed, covering several relevant aspects but not all (e.g., ecological trade-offs).  This constitutes *some* evaluation, meeting band 2.

The report follows the IB IA structure with clear headings, tables and figures; however, formatting glitches, duplicated figure captions and occasional disorganisation interrupt flow, so structure is present but not consistently sustainedâband 2.

A strengths/weaknesses table identifies data breadth and source reliability versus secondary-data limits and spatial heterogeneity, satisfying the âdescribesâ level.  Discussion is present but not exhaustive (no mention of statistical assumptions or alternative tests), so 3 is awarded.

The introduction discusses wildfire frequency, PM2.5 toxicity and asthma impacts, cites authoritative agencies and integrates local anecdote, providing a clear environmental context for the research.  The discussion goes beyond a simple outline by explaining why PM2.5 from fires is a public-health issue in California and why the period chosen matters; thus it meets the âdiscussesâ level.  Occasional repetition prevents a full 6.

The student *describes* choosing California-wide averages and a 21-year time series but does not justify why this sampling frame is optimal, nor address representativeness or potential spatial bias.  Description without explicit justification places the work in the 3â4 band; limited depth merits the lower mark of 3.

Ethical/risk issues are mentioned only briefly (obligation to use accurate secondary data); physical, digital-security or ethical risks are not systematically analysed.  The attempt is a simple list with omissions, matching the 1â2 band; some acknowledgment of ethics justifies the top of that band (2).

All raw data are tabulated and each variable pair is graphed with labelled axes and trendlines.  Minor issues (missing axis titles, small blurry images, inconsistent figure numbering) mean some omissions exist, so the strand fits the 3â4 band; breadth of presentation warrants the upper mark (4).

One applicationâpromoting fire-resistant plantsâis *described* with reference to literature and some justification (reduced fire intensity), fulfilling the band 2 descriptor.  The rationale ties partially to findings but lacks explicit quantitative linkage, so 2 marks.

The argument generally progresses from context to results to evaluation, but some sections (especially figures 8â11 description) are confusing, and transitions are weak.  Hence the presentation is âmainly logical but difficult to follow in partsâ, fitting band 2.

annotations.comment: The significance levels are represented by symbols without a legend; clarify symbol meaning and ensure readers can interpret which level is selected.
Table 1 lacks a descriptive title and context; add a table caption summarizing contents and refer to it in-text for clearer presentation.
The Pearson r score is presented as 0.509 in text but shown as 0.500 in the calculator form; reconcile these discrepancies to ensure accurate reporting.
Embedding an interactive form disrupts flow; replace with a static screenshot or replicate results in your own graph for coherent presentation.
Figure 5 explanation repeats equation usage guidance rather than focusing on result interpretation; revise to discuss observed trends and statistical significance.
The third column header has inconsistent capitalization and spacing ('current Particulate matter PM2.5 levels'); standardize header formatting for professionalism and readability.
The research question is precise and clearly identifies variables, location, and timeframe. However the duplicated phrasing is redundant; consider consolidating to improve clarity.
The title provides a general topic but could be more specific by incorporating key variables (wildfire area, PM2.5, asthma rates) to reflect the research scope.

The introduction relies on a quote from New York State Department of Health to discuss PM2.5 impacts; consider sourcing state-specific data (e.g., California Air Resources Board) for stronger contextual relevance.
Personal anecdote adds narrative interest but weakens academic tone; consider summarizing experience more formally or relocating to a brief contextual note.
This introductory phrase is unnecessary and interrupts flow; merge directly into the context statement to improve cohesion.
The background relies on extensive direct quotations; paraphrasing and synthesizing key points would demonstrate deeper understanding and improve readability.
Expand the link between climate changeâdriven drought conditions and increased wildfire frequency to clarify how this underpins your correlational study design.
Figure 1 lacks axis labels and a detailed caption; include titles for both axes and specify data source directly under the figure to ensure clarity and reproducibility.

The variables description conflates PM2.5 as a dependent variable alongside asthma rates; specify asthma rate as the dependent variable and PM2.5 as an explanatory intermediate for methodological clarity.
These items describe data source and scope rather than control variables; identify actual controls (e.g., seasonal variability, demographic factors) to mitigate confounding effects in analysis.
Method description omits discussion of statistical assumptions (e.g., normality, linearity) necessary for Pearson correlation; include these to justify test choice and ensure validity.
Ethical and data-security considerations are missing; discuss potential privacy issues related to health data and acknowledge how data integrity was ensured.
Table includes mixed formatting for decimal places and lacks units for PM2.5 header; standardize numerical precision and clearly state units in column headings.
The narrative for Figure 2 should explain all symbols in Equation 1 explicitly (e.g., define xi, yi, \bar{x}, \bar{y}) before presenting results to aid reader comprehension.
Equation notation uses \bar{v} for PM2.5 mean instead of the expected \bar{y}; maintain consistent symbols between formula and text to avoid confusion.
The P-value is reported with six decimals then rounded to three without noting rounding rules; state rounding conventions and maintain consistency to support statistical interpretation.
No interpretation is provided for the non-significant correlation between PM2.5 and asthma rates; discuss potential reasons (e.g., confounders) to strengthen analysis.
The text refers to Figure 7 but the numbering in figure headings is inconsistent; ensure sequential, consistent figure labels throughout the report.
Has the student considered converting this HTML form element into a static graphic? Unprocessed HTML may not render correctly in printed IA copies.
